questions.text: Resistive forces are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Non-conservative forces are those that do dissipate energy as an object moves through a closed path. The work done by a non-conservative force depends on the path taken by the object. Friction and air resistance are typical examples of non-conservative forces. Now, resistive forces, such as friction and air resistance, fall into the category of non-conservative forces. These forces lead to the dissipation of energy in the system as an object moves, and the work done by these forces is path dependent.

questions.explanation: Non-conservative forces are those that do dissipate energy as an object moves through a closed path. The work done by a non-conservative force depends on the path taken by the object. Friction and air resistance are typical examples of non-conservative forces. Now, resistive forces, such as friction and air resistance, fall into the category of non-conservative forces. These forces lead to the dissipation of energy in the system as an object moves, and the work done by these forces is path dependent.

questions.text: The current through a metallic conductor is due to the motion of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The current in a metallic conductor is primarily due to the motion of free electrons. These electrons are loosely bound and can move through the lattice structure of the metal.
Protons are positively charged particles and are not free to move in a conductor
Neutrons are neutral and do not carry any charge, so they do not contribute to current.
The motion of free electrons causing current in metallic conductors is a well-established concept in physics.

questions.explanation: The current in a metallic conductor is primarily due to the motion of free electrons. These electrons are loosely bound and can move through the lattice structure of the metal.

questions.text: Resistance of a conductor depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Resistance is independent of the charge.
Resistance depends on current, but it is not a direct dependency; it is related through Ohm's Law, R = V/I.
Resistance is related to power through the formula P = I²R, but it is not the primary factor affecting resistance.

The resistance of a conductor depends on its temperature according to the formula R = R₀(1 + αΔT), where R₀ is the initial resistance, α is the temperature coefficient of the material, and ΔT is the change in temperature.

questions.explanation: The resistance of a conductor depends on its temperature according to the formula R = R₀(1 + αΔT), where R₀ is the initial resistance, α is the temperature coefficient of the material, and ΔT is the change in temperature.

questions.text: A car battery is rated at 80A•11. An ampere-hour is a unit of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Ampere-hour is not a measure of the conductivity of a material.
Similar to choice (a), ampere-hour is not related to the conductivity.
Ampere-hour is not a unit used to measure the properties of insulators.
An ampere-hour (A•h) is a unit of electric charge, representing the amount of charge transferred by a current of one ampere in one hour.

questions.explanation: An ampere-hour (A•h) is a unit of electric charge, representing the amount of charge transferred by a current of one ampere in one hour.

questions.text: A wire of uniform area of cross-section 'A' length 'L' and resistance R is cut into two parts. Resistivity of each part
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Resistivity (ρ) is an intrinsic property of a material and remains constant. The resistance of each part will change based on the length and area of cross-section.
Cutting the wire doesn't change the resistivity of the material.
Same as explanation for (b).
Resistivity does not become zero unless the material itself changes.

questions.explanation: Resistivity (ρ) is an intrinsic property of a material and remains constant. The resistance of each part will change based on the length and area of cross-section.

questions.text: Current is a measure of
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: force that moves a charge past a point
options.explanation: This describes electric field, not current.

options.text: amount of charge that moves past a point per unit time
options.explanation: Current (I) is defined as the rate of flow of charge, i.e., I = ΔQ/Δt.

options.text: resistance to the movement of a charge past a point
options.explanation: This describes resistance, not current.

options.text: energy used to move a charge past a point
options.explanation: This relates to power, not current.

questions.explanation: Current (I) is defined as the rate of flow of charge, i.e., I = ΔQ/Δt.

questions.text: When the same current passes for the same time through a thick and thin wire:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: more heat is produced in the thick wire
options.explanation: ﻿The thicker wire has lower resistance, leading to less heat production.

options.text: more heat is produced in the thin wire
options.explanation: According to the formula for electrical power (P = I²R), where R is the resistance, the thin wire will have higher resistance, resulting in more heat produced as compared to the thick wire.

options.text: no heat is produced in the wire
options.explanation: Heat is produced whenever current flows through a conductor.

options.text: Equal heat in thick and thin wire
options.explanation: Heat produced depends on resistance, and since the resistance is different for thick and thin wires, the heat produced will not be equal.

questions.explanation: According to the formula for electrical power (P = I²R), where R is the resistance, the thin wire will have higher resistance, resulting in more heat produced as compared to the thick wire.

questions.subject: Physics

Incorrect calculation, as Q = I * t, not I / t.
The data is sufficient, and the calculation can be performed using the given current and time.

questions.explanation: The charge (Q) flowing through a conductor is given by Q = I * t, where I is the current and t is the time.

questions.text: One kilowatt-hour is the amount of energy delivered during

options.explanation: kWh involves a time component of one hour, not one second.
kWh involves a time component of one hour, not one day.
kWh involves a time component of one hour, not one minute.
One kilowatt-hour (kWh) is a unit of energy, and it represents the amount of energy consumed or delivered when one kilowatt of power is used for one hour.

questions.explanation: One kilowatt-hour (kWh) is a unit of energy, and it represents the amount of energy consumed or delivered when one kilowatt of power is used for one hour.

questions.text: Thermocouples convert
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Thermocouples generate an electromotive force (EMF) in response to a temperature difference, converting heat energy into electrical energy.
Thermocouples do not produce light; they generate electrical energy.
Thermocouples do not directly produce mechanical energy.
This is not the process involved in thermocouples.

questions.explanation: Thermocouples generate an electromotive force (EMF) in response to a temperature difference, converting heat energy into electrical energy.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Using Ohm's Law (R = V²/P), where R is resistance, V is voltage, and P is power, the resistance of the light bulb can be calculated as 240 ohms.
Incorrect calculation, using the correct formula gives a different value
Incorrect calculation, using the correct formula gives a different value.
Incorrect calculation, using the correct formula gives a different value.

questions.explanation: Using Ohm's Law (R = V²/P), where R is resistance, V is voltage, and P is power, the resistance of the light bulb can be calculated as 240 ohms.

questions.text: In the diagram R1>R2>R3. Rank the three resistors according to the current in them, least to greatest.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Resistors are said to be connected in “Series“, when they are daisy chained together in a single line. Since all the current flowing through the first resistor has no other way to go it must also pass through the second resistor and the third and so on. Then, resistors in series have a Common Current flowing through them as the current that flows through one resistor must also flow through the others as it can only take one path.Now here R1,R2 and R3 are in series. So the current flowing through them are same irrespective of the resistance values. So option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Resistors are said to be connected in “Series“, when they are daisy chained together in a single line. Since all the current flowing through the first resistor has no other way to go it must also pass through the second resistor and the third and so on. Then, resistors in series have a Common Current flowing through them as the current that flows through one resistor must also flow through the others as it can only take one path.Now here R1,R2 and R3 are in series. So the current flowing through them are same irrespective of the resistance values. So option C is correct.

options.explanation: This value is too small and not realistic for the current caused by the motion of electrons in an atom

The current (I) caused by the motion of electrons in orbit is given by the formula I = frac{Q}{t}), where (Q) is the charge and (t) is the time taken to complete one orbit.The charge of an electron is (e approx 1.6 * 10^{-19}C). The current is then calculated as (I = frac{e}{t}). Substituting the given values, we get (I = frac{1.6 * 10^{-19} C}{1.0 * 10^{-15} s} = 1.6 * 10^{4} A)
This value is too large and not realistic for the current caused by the motion of electrons in an atom.

questions.explanation: The current (I) caused by the motion of electrons in orbit is given by the formula I = frac{Q}{t}), where (Q) is the charge and (t) is the time taken to complete one orbit.The charge of an electron is (e approx 1.6 * 10^{-19}C). The current is then calculated as (I = frac{e}{t}). Substituting the given values, we get (I = frac{1.6 * 10^{-19} C}{1.0 * 10^{-15} s} = 1.6 * 10^{4} A)

questions.text: Reciprocal of resistivity is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Reciprocal of resistivity is conductance, not resistance
Inductance is a property related to inductors, not resistivity
The reciprocal of resistivity is conductivity (σ), and it is given by the formula σ=1/ ρ, where ρ is resistivity.
Flexibility is not a term associated with the reciprocal of resistivity

questions.explanation: The reciprocal of resistivity is conductivity (σ), and it is given by the formula σ=1/ ρ, where ρ is resistivity.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Let R1 = 2Ω R2 = 4Ω R3 = 6ΩFor resistance connected in parallel combination, the equivalent resistance will be:1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R31/Req = 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/6= 11/12 Ω

questions.explanation: Let R1 = 2Ω R2 = 4Ω R3 = 6ΩFor resistance connected in parallel combination, the equivalent resistance will be:1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R31/Req = 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/6= 11/12 Ω

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Not all complex networks provide continuously varying potential.
Wheatstone bridge is a specific arrangement for measuring resistance, not continuously varying potential

Only (c) potential divider is specifically related to continuously varying potential.

questions.explanation: A potential divider, or voltage divider, is a circuit that divides the voltage across its components, providing a continuously varying potential.

questions.text: Internal resistance is the resistance offered by
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Internal resistance refers to the resistance offered by the source of electromotive force (e.m.f), such as a battery.
Conductor resistance is not referred to as internal resistance.
Internal resistance is associated with the source, not an external resistor.
Capacitors do not have internal resistance; they have reactance in AC circuits.

questions.explanation: Internal resistance refers to the resistance offered by the source of electromotive force (e.m.f), such as a battery.

The power of a bulb is related to the thickness of its filament. Higher power bulbs generally have thicker filaments to handle the increased current and heat.
Generally has a thinner filament than 100W and 200W.
All bulbs do not have the same filament thickness; it depends on their power.

questions.explanation: The power of a bulb is related to the thickness of its filament. Higher power bulbs generally have thicker filaments to handle the increased current and heat.

questions.text: The current in the circuit shown in the figure. What will be the current in the circuit?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Given,Voltage = 2Vr1 = 30Ωr2 = 30Ωr3 = 30ΩTo find,The current in the circuitSolution,The current in the circuit shown in the figure is 0.1 A.We can simply find the current in the circuit by using the formula ofV = IRr1 and r2 are connected in series so the resistance would be r4 = r1+r2r4 (in series) =30+30 = 60Ωr3 and r4 are connected in parallel so resistance would be1/r5 = 1/r3+1/r4 = 1/60+1/30 = 3/60 = 1/20 r5 = 20Ω I = V/R = 2/20 = 1/10 = 0.1 AHence, The current in the circuit shown in the figure is 0.1 A.

questions.explanation: Given,Voltage = 2Vr1 = 30Ωr2 = 30Ωr3 = 30ΩTo find,The current in the circuitSolution,The current in the circuit shown in the figure is 0.1 A.We can simply find the current in the circuit by using the formula ofV = IRr1 and r2 are connected in series so the resistance would be r4 = r1+r2r4 (in series) =30+30 = 60Ωr3 and r4 are connected in parallel so resistance would be1/r5 = 1/r3+1/r4 = 1/60+1/30 = 3/60 = 1/20 r5 = 20Ω I = V/R = 2/20 = 1/10 = 0.1 AHence, The current in the circuit shown in the figure is 0.1 A.

questions.text: Resistance of a wire on increasing its temperature will
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The resistance of a wire generally increases with an increase in temperature. This phenomenon is described by the temperature coefficient of resistance α.
In most cases, resistance increases with temperature due to increased collision of electrons with lattice vibrations.
This is generally not the case; resistance typically changes with temperature.
Altitude may affect experiments, but the primary factor here is temperature.

questions.explanation: The resistance of a wire generally increases with an increase in temperature. This phenomenon is described by the temperature coefficient of resistance α.

questions.text: In liquids and gasses, the current is due to the motion
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Wrong. In liquids and gasses, it's not only negative charges (electrons) but also positive charges (ions) that contribute to current
Wrong. Similarly, positive charges (ions) also play a role in the current in liquids and gasses.

In liquids and gasses, the current is due to the motion of both negative and positive charges. In these mediums, both electrons (negative charges) and ions (positive charges) can move, contributing to the flow of electric current
Wrong. Neutral particles do not carry a net electric charge and, therefore, do not contribute to electric current in these mediums

questions.explanation: In liquids and gasses, the current is due to the motion of both negative and positive charges. In these mediums, both electrons (negative charges) and ions (positive charges) can move, contributing to the flow of electric current

questions.text: A 100W ,200V bulb is connected to a 160V supply. The actual power consumption would be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect: This option doesn't consider the decrease in power due to the reduction in voltage.

The power consumed by the bulb can be calculated using the formula P=V2/R, where P is power, V is voltage and R is resistance. In this case, with 160V supply instead of the designed 200V, the new power consumption is 1602/2002 x 100W= 72W
Incorrect: This option assumes that the power remains the same despite the change in voltage.
Incorrect: Similar to option (C), it does not account for the change in power due to decrease in voltage.

questions.explanation: The power consumed by the bulb can be calculated using the formula P=V2/R, where P is power, V is voltage and R is resistance. In this case, with 160V supply instead of the designed 200V, the new power consumption is 1602/2002 x 100W= 72W

questions.text: The graphical representation of Ohm's law is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect: Ohm's law does not have a hyperbolic relationship between voltage and current.
Incorrect: Ohm's law does not have an elliptical relationship between voltage and current.
Incorrect: Ohm's law does not have a parabolic relationship between voltage and current.

The graphical representation of Ohm's law is a straight line. Ohm's law states that the current (I) flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage (V) across it, given a constant resistance (R). Mathematically, I=V/R, and when plotted on a graph with V on the x-axis and I on the y-axis, the resulting graph is a straight line.

questions.explanation: The graphical representation of Ohm's law is a straight line. Ohm's law states that the current (I) flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage (V) across it, given a constant resistance (R). Mathematically, I=V/R, and when plotted on a graph with V on the x-axis and I on the y-axis, the resulting graph is a straight line.

questions.text: SI unit of resistivity is_
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option (a) is for ohm-meter squared and is used to represent the resistivity of a area
Option (b) is an incorrect option.

The property of a material determines how strongly it can resist the flow of electric current. The formula for Resistivity is given by ρ= R x A/LHere ρ is the resistivity of the conductor, R is the resistance of conductor, A is the cross-sectional area of the conductor, L is the length of the conductor. By putting SI units of these quantities in the formula:ρ= Ω x m2/mρ= Ω x mThus, SI unit of Resistivity is ρ= Ω.m
Option (d) represents the reciprocal of the resistivity. This unit is equivalent to siemens per meter (S/m), where the siemens (S) is the unit of electrical conductance.

questions.explanation: The property of a material determines how strongly it can resist the flow of electric current. The formula for Resistivity is given by ρ= R x A/LHere ρ is the resistivity of the conductor, R is the resistance of conductor, A is the cross-sectional area of the conductor, L is the length of the conductor. By putting SI units of these quantities in the formula:ρ= Ω x m2/mρ= Ω x mThus, SI unit of Resistivity is ρ= Ω.m

questions.text: The lower part of a cloud has positive, The cloud discharges in flash of lightening, in which direction do electrons and conventional current flow:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect: This option incorrectly suggests that both electrons and conventional current move downward.
Incorrect: This option incorrectly suggests that electrons move upward, which is not the case during a lightning flash.

In a lightning flash, the lower part of the cloud becomes negatively charged. Electrons (which are negatively charged) move downward from the cloud to the ground, creating a path for the discharge. The conventional current, however, is considered to flow in the opposite direction of the electron flow. So, from the ground perspective, it appears as if conventional current is moving upward.
Incorrect: This option incorrectly suggests that both electrons and conventional current move upward.

questions.explanation: In a lightning flash, the lower part of the cloud becomes negatively charged. Electrons (which are negatively charged) move downward from the cloud to the ground, creating a path for the discharge. The conventional current, however, is considered to flow in the opposite direction of the electron flow. So, from the ground perspective, it appears as if conventional current is moving upward.

questions.text: A fuse is placed in series with the circuit to protect against:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. Fuses are not primarily designed to protect against high power but rather high current.
Incorrect. Fuses are not primarily designed to protect against high voltage but rather high current.

A fuse is placed in series with a circuit to protect against high current. The primary purpose of a fuse is to interrupt the circuit when the current exceeds a certain specified value. This helps prevent damage to the circuit components and reduces the risk of overheating, fire, or other hazards associated with excessive current.
Incorrect. While a fuse can indirectly protect against overheating by interrupting the circuit when there's a high current, its primary purpose is to safeguard against excessive current

questions.explanation: A fuse is placed in series with a circuit to protect against high current. The primary purpose of a fuse is to interrupt the circuit when the current exceeds a certain specified value. This helps prevent damage to the circuit components and reduces the risk of overheating, fire, or other hazards associated with excessive current.

Incorrect. 2C is not the correct amount of charge that flows through the conductor in 1 hour with a 1-ampere current.

questions.text: A and B are two points in an electric field. If the work done in carrying 4.0 coulombs of electric charge from A to B is 16.0 joules, the potential difference between A and B is

options.explanation: The potential difference (V) is equal to the work done (W) per unit charge (Q), i.e., V=W/Q​. Given that work done is 16.0 J and charge is 4.0 C, V=16.0/4.0​=4.0 V.Incorrect options: (a) If there was no potential difference, no work would be done in moving the charge.(b) This calculation is incorrect based on the given work and charge.(d) This value is not consistent with the given work and charge.

options.text: depends on the system of units only
depends on the medium between the charges only
depends on both the system of units and the medium between the charges

options.explanation: The constant k in Coulomb's law is known as the Coulomb's constant and has a value of 8.9875×10^9N m^2/C^2. It is independent of the system of units used and the medium between the charges.Incorrect options: (a) and (b) Coulomb's constant is a universal constant and is not dependent on either the system of units or the medium between the charges.(c) Coulomb's constant is independent of both the system of units and the medium between the charges.

questions.text: Electrical energy used per second is given by

options.explanation: The electrical power P is given by P=VI, where V is the voltage and I is the current. Additionally, power can also be expressed as P=I^2R and P=V^2/R using Ohm's law (V=IR). Therefore, all of the options (a), (b), and (c) are correct representations of electrical energy used per second.Incorrect options: None. All options are correct.

questions.text: Three resistances, each of 4 Ω, are connected to form a triangle. The resistance between any two terminals is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When three equal resistances are connected to form a triangle, the equivalent resistance between any two terminals is twice the resistance of each resistor. Therefore, Req​=2*R=2×4Ω=8Ω. However, this resistance is shared by 3 paths. The effective resistance between any two terminals is 1/3 of this, which is 8/3​ ​Ω.Incorrect options: (a) This doesn't accurately reflect the calculation for the equivalent resistance.(b) This would be the case if the resistances were connected in series, not in the triangle configuration.(d) This is not the correct calculation for the resistance in the triangle configuration.

questions.text: A student has 10 resistors, each of resistance r. The minimum resistance that can be obtained by him using these resistors is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To obtain the minimum resistance, the resistors should be connected in parallel. The formula for the total resistance of resistors connected in parallel is 1/Rtotal​​=1/R1​+1/R2​​+…+1/Rn​​. Substituting R1​=R2​=…=R10​=r, we get 1/Rtotal​​=10/r​, hence Rtotal​=r/10​.Incorrect options: (a), (b), and (d) These options don't correctly represent the calculation for the minimum resistance of resistors connected in parallel.

Substituting R1​=R2​=…=R10​=r, we get 1/Rtotal​​=10/r​, hence Rtotal​=r/10​.Incorrect options: (a), (b), and (d) These options don't correctly represent the calculation for the minimum resistance of resistors connected in parallel.

questions.text: Resistive forces are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Non-conservative forces are those that dissipate energy as an object moves through a closed path. The work done by a non-conservative force depends on the path taken by the object. Friction and air resistance are typical examples of non-conservative forces. Now, resistive forces, such as friction and air resistance, fall into the category of non-conservative forces. These forces lead to the dissipation of energy in the system as an object moves, and the work done by these forces is path-dependent.

questions.text: Four resistors A, B, C, and D form a Wheatstone bridge. The bridge is balanced when C = 100 Ω. If A and B are interchanged, the bridge balances for C = 121 Ω. The value of D is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a balanced Wheatstone bridge, the voltage across the resistors C and D is zero. This means that the ratio of the resistances of the two legs of the bridge must be equal.Let R_A and R_B(underscore value represents subscript) be the resistances of resistors A and B, respectively. When the bridge is balanced with C = 100 Ω, we have the following equation:R_A / R_B = D / 100When A and B are interchanged, the bridge balances for C = 121 Ω, so we have the following equation:R_B / R_A = D / 121We can solve these two equations simultaneously to find the values of R_A, R_B, and D.First, we can rearrange the first equation to get an expression for R\_B:R_B = R_A (D / 100)Next, we can substitute this expression into the second equation to get an equation that only involves R_A and D:R_A / R_A (D / 100) = D / 121Simplifying this equation, we get:1 / (D / 100) = D / 121Cross-multiplying and solving for D, we get:D^2 = 100 x 121D = sqrt(100 x 121)D ≈ 110 ΩTherefore, the value of resistor D is approximately 110 Ω.Note: This answer assumes that the resistances of the resistors are exact values and that the bridge is perfectly balanced. In practice, there may be small errors due to tolerances in the resistors and imperfections in the bridge, so the actual value of D may be slightly different

questions.explanation: In a balanced Wheatstone bridge, the voltage across the resistors C and D is zero. This means that the ratio of the resistances of the two legs of the bridge must be equal.Let R_A and R_B(underscore value represents subscript) be the resistances of resistors A and B, respectively. When the bridge is balanced with C = 100 Ω, we have the following equation:R_A / R_B = D / 100When A and B are interchanged, the bridge balances for C = 121 Ω, so we have the following equation:R_B / R_A = D / 121We can solve these two equations simultaneously to find the values of R_A, R_B, and D.First, we can rearrange the first equation to get an expression for R\_B:R_B = R_A (D / 100)Next, we can substitute this expression into the second equation to get an equation that only involves R_A and D:R_A / R_A (D / 100) = D / 121Simplifying this equation, we get:1 / (D / 100) = D / 121Cross-multiplying and solving for D, we get:D^2 = 100 x 121D = sqrt(100 x 121)D ≈ 110 ΩTherefore, the value of resistor D is approximately 110 Ω.Note: This answer assumes that the resistances of the resistors are exact values and that the bridge is perfectly balanced. In practice, there may be small errors due to tolerances in the resistors and imperfections in the bridge, so the actual value of D may be slightly different

questions.text: A wire of uniform cross section A , length 1 and resistance R is cut into two equal pieces. The resistivity of each piece will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Resistivity is a fundamental property of a material that describes its opposition to the flow of electric current. It is an intrinsic property, meaning it depends on the material's inherent nature and does not change with the shape or size of the conductor.The formula for resistance (R) is:R = ρ * (l / A)where:ρ (rho) is the resistivity of the material (measured in ohm-meters, Ω⋅m)l is the length of the conductor (meters, m)A is the cross-sectional area of the conductor (square meters, m²)In the given scenario, the wire is cut into two equal pieces. Since the resistivity (ρ) is a property of the material and doesn't change with the size or shape of the conductor, each of the two pieces will have the same resistivity as the original wire.

Since the resistivity (ρ) is a property of the material and doesn't change with the size or shape of the conductor, each of the two pieces will have the same resistivity as the original wire.

questions.text: the metalic wires are lying parallel. if the current in these wires be flowing in same direction, the wires will
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, they attract each other (a). This phenomenon occurs due to the magnetic fields generated by the current flow.Here's the explanation:Current flow creates magnetic field: When a current flows through a conductor, it creates a magnetic field around the conductor. The direction and strength of this magnetic field are determined by the right-hand rule.Magnetic fields interact: When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, the magnetic fields they create interact with each other. In this specific case, the magnetic field lines generated by each wire loop in the same direction around the other wire.Right-hand rule again: Applying the right-hand rule for magnetic forces, the fingers of your right hand curl in the direction of the current flow, and your thumb points in the direction of the force exerted by the magnetic field. In this case, the magnetic fields of each wire exert a force towards the other wire, resulting in an attraction.

options.text: have no force of attraction or repulsion

This phenomenon occurs due to the magnetic fields generated by the current flow.Here's the explanation:Current flow creates magnetic field: When a current flows through a conductor, it creates a magnetic field around the conductor. The direction and strength of this magnetic field are determined by the right-hand rule.Magnetic fields interact: When two parallel wires carry current in the same direction, the magnetic fields they create interact with each other. In this specific case, the magnetic field lines generated by each wire loop in the same direction around the other wire.Right-hand rule again: Applying the right-hand rule for magnetic forces, the fingers of your right hand curl in the direction of the current flow, and your thumb points in the direction of the force exerted by the magnetic field. In this case, the magnetic fields of each wire exert a force towards the other wire, resulting in an attraction.

questions.text: a wire of radius r has resistances R. if is stretched to a wire of r/2 radius, then the resistance becomes
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Resistance of a wire is given by R = ρL/AWhere, ρ is resistivity of material of wire (constant)L is length of wireA is cross-sectional area of wireFor original wire: Area (A) = πr^2For stretched wire: Area (A') = π(r/2)^2 = πr^2/4Length (L) remains same for both wiresUsing the formula:Resistance of original wire = R = ρL/πr^2Resistance of stretched wire = R' = ρL/πr^2/4R'/R = πr^2/πr^2/4 = 4Therefore, the resistance of the stretched wire becomes 4 times the original resistance

questions.explanation: Resistance of a wire is given by R = ρL/AWhere, ρ is resistivity of material of wire (constant)L is length of wireA is cross-sectional area of wireFor original wire: Area (A) = πr^2For stretched wire: Area (A') = π(r/2)^2 = πr^2/4Length (L) remains same for both wiresUsing the formula:Resistance of original wire = R = ρL/πr^2Resistance of stretched wire = R' = ρL/πr^2/4R'/R = πr^2/πr^2/4 = 4Therefore, the resistance of the stretched wire becomes 4 times the original resistance

questions.text: a uniform resistance wire of length L and diameter d has a resistance R. another wire of same material has length, 4Lnand diameter 2d, the resistance will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: We can find the new resistance of the second wire by considering the relationship between resistance, length, and cross-sectional area in a wire made of the same material.Resistance Formula:The resistance (R) of a wire is related to its resistivity (ρ), length (L), and cross-sectional area (A) by the formula:R = ρ * (L / A)Given information:First wire: Length (L1) = L, Diameter (d1) = d, Resistance (R1) = RSecond wire: Length (L2) = 4L, Diameter (d2) = 2dCalculate Cross-sectional area:The area is calculated as π * (diameter^2) / 4.First wire: A1 = π * (d1^2) / 4Second wire: A2 = π * (d2^2) / 4 = π * (2d)^2 / 4 = 4 * π * (d^2) / 4 (since d2 = 2d)Find the new resistance (R2):Substitute the values for the second wire into the formula:R2 = ρ * (L2 / A2)R2 = ρ * (4L / (4 * π * (d^2) / 4))Simplify the equation:Since the resistivity (ρ) remains the same for both wires, we can cancel out:R2 = ρ * (L / (π * (d^2) / 4))This equation is exactly the same as the formula for the first wire's resistance (R1) with L = L and A = A1.Therefore, the new resistance (R2) of the second wire is equal to the original resistance (R) of the first wire (b).

questions.explanation: We can find the new resistance of the second wire by considering the relationship between resistance, length, and cross-sectional area in a wire made of the same material.Resistance Formula:The resistance (R) of a wire is related to its resistivity (ρ), length (L), and cross-sectional area (A) by the formula:R = ρ * (L / A)Given information:First wire: Length (L1) = L, Diameter (d1) = d, Resistance (R1) = RSecond wire: Length (L2) = 4L, Diameter (d2) = 2dCalculate Cross-sectional area:The area is calculated as π * (diameter^2) / 4.First wire: A1 = π * (d1^2) / 4Second wire: A2 = π * (d2^2) / 4 = π * (2d)^2 / 4 = 4 * π * (d^2) / 4 (since d2 = 2d)Find the new resistance (R2):Substitute the values for the second wire into the formula:R2 = ρ * (L2 / A2)R2 = ρ * (4L / (4 * π * (d^2) / 4))Simplify the equation:Since the resistivity (ρ) remains the same for both wires, we can cancel out:R2 = ρ * (L / (π * (d^2) / 4))This equation is exactly the same as the formula for the first wire's resistance (R1) with L = L and A = A1.Therefore, the new resistance (R2) of the second wire is equal to the original resistance (R) of the first wire (b).

questions.text: Which material is commonly used as an electrical conductor in daily use?

options.explanation: Copper is the most common material used as an electrical conductor in daily use. Copper has high electrical conductivity, making it an excellent choice for various electrical applications such as wiring, cables, and electrical components.The other options are less suitable as common electrical conductors
Aluminium also has good electrical conductivity but is not as widely used as copper in daily applications

questions.text: In modern day electrical arrangements, which of the following is connected in series with the others?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In modern electrical arrangements, appliances and devices are typically connected in parallel, not in series. Here's why:Parallel Connection: In a parallel circuit, each appliance has its own separate path for current to flow. This means that if one appliance fails, the others will continue to operate normally. Additionally, each appliance receives the full voltage of the power source, ensuring consistent performance.Series Connection: In a series circuit, all appliances share the same path for current flow. If one appliance fails, the entire circuit breaks, and all appliances stop working. Furthermore, the voltage is divided among the appliances in a series circuit, which can lead to inconsistent performance.Therefore, in modern electrical systems, appliances are connected in parallel to ensure reliability and consistent operation.

If one appliance fails, the entire circuit breaks, and all appliances stop working. Furthermore, the voltage is divided among the appliances in a series circuit, which can lead to inconsistent performance.Therefore, in modern electrical systems, appliances are connected in parallel to ensure reliability and consistent operation.
